Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | Source name: LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance | Definition: The most common type of medical expense insurance, which pays benefits for a wide range of expenses including hospital, surgical, physician, prescription drugs, and some preventive care. | Domain Tags: medical expense, comprehensive coverage | Related Nodes: KN-002, KN-006, KN-007, KN-008, KN-009 | Category: Insurance Product | Node Name: Major Medical Coverage | Chunk Text Content: Module 2: Individual Insurance Products
Learning Objectives
After studying this lesson, you will be able to:

Describe the benefits provided under major medical and supplemental
medical expense insurance policies.

Explain common cost participation methods.

Explain how managed care plans attempt to manage members’ access
to medical care and the costs of that care.

Describe the basic characteristics of consumer-driven health plans.

Describe the benefit triggers for long-term care insurance policies.

payload.summary: LOMA 281 Module 2 Lesson 4 Health Insurance Summary of Key Points Health insurance protects against the risk of financial loss from illness or injury. Available in three main forms—medical expense, long-term care, and disability income. Medical expense coverage pays for treatment of insured’s illnesses and injuries. • Traditional medical expense coverage o Provides benefits for basic medical care; excludes some expenses o Require insureds to participate in medical costs—deductible, coinsurance • Supplemental benefits offered through dental and vision coverage, as well as dread disease (critical illness) coverage • Managed care plans manage members’ healthcare and costs o Use provider networks and primary care providers to coordinate care and access to specialists o Three types of plans: health maintenance organization (HMO), preferred provider organization (PPO), and point-of-service (POS) plan o Require insureds to participate in medical costs—copayment o Offer preventive, wellness, and patient education programs • Consumer-driven health plans encourage insureds to manage their own health care costs o Health savings plans, health reimbursement arrangements • The Affordable Care Act (ACA) Long-term care (LTC) coverage pays for medical and other services for constant care at home or in a qualified facility • Benefit triggers include activities of daily living (ADLs) and cognitive impairment • A variety of qualified facilities • Most policies include a daily benefit amount; others include a monthly benefit amount Disability income coverage provides income replacement benefits if insured is unable to work because of illness or injury. • Three common definitions of total disability: o Current usual definition, used in most policies o Own previous occupation definition o Income loss definition • Presumptive disabilities include loss of speech or hearing, total and permanent blindness, and loss of the use of any two limbs • Specify an elimination period (waiting period) before benefits are payable • Disability income payments are less than insured’s pre-disability earnings • Supplemental benefits available, including partial disability benefits and cost-of- living adjustments (COLAs) • Disability benefits through government-sponsored programs o Supplemental Security Income (SSI) program based on financial need o Social Security Disability Income (SSDI) program based on Social Security taxes paid o Workers’ Compensation program based on illness or injury suffered on the job Copyright © 2020 LOMA (Life Office Management Association, Inc.). All rights reserved.

coverage—generally pays benefits for

Basic hospital, surgical, and physicians' expenses

Additional services related to illness or injury


share in the costs of some types of medical care.

Deductible—a flat dollar amount of eligible expenses that the insured

Deductible example
Most major medical policies include a calendar-year deductible. For example,
a policy with a $500 calendar-year deductible requires that the insured
incur $500 of covered medical expenses in one calendar year before the
insurance policy will begin payments.

Coinsurance—a percentage of the allowable expenses remaining after
the insured has paid the deductible that must be paid by the insured.
Coinsurance example

Coinsurance example
Major medical policies often include a 20 to 30 percent coinsurance
requirement. For example, a policy with a $500 annual deductible and a
20% coinsurance requirement would require that the insured pay 20% of all
charges above $500.

payload.summary: LOMA 281 Module 2 Lesson 4 Health Insurance Summary of Key Points Health insurance protects against the risk of financial loss from illness or injury. Available in three main forms—medical expense, long-term care, and disability income. Medical expense coverage pays for treatment of insured’s illnesses and injuries. • Traditional medical expense coverage o Provides benefits for basic medical care; excludes some expenses o Require insureds to participate in medical costs—deductible, coinsurance • Supplemental benefits offered through dental and vision coverage, as well as dread disease (critical illness) coverage • Managed care plans manage members’ healthcare and costs o Use provider networks and primary care providers to coordinate care and access to specialists o Three types of plans: health maintenance organization (HMO), preferred provider organization (PPO), and point-of-service (POS) plan o Require insureds to participate in medical costs—copayment o Offer preventive, wellness, and patient education programs • Consumer-driven health plans encourage insureds to manage their own health care costs o Health savings plans, health reimbursement arrangements • The Affordable Care Act (ACA) Long-term care (LTC) coverage pays for medical and other services for constant care at home or in a qualified facility • Benefit triggers include activities of daily living (ADLs) and cognitive impairment • A variety of qualified facilities • Most policies include a daily benefit amount; Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | Source name: LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance | Definition: A specified, fixed dollar amount that a member of a managed care plan must pay a network provider each time they receive a service. | Domain Tags: cost sharing, fixed fee, copay | Related Nodes: KN-006, KN-012 | Category: Cost Participation Method | Node Name: Copayment | Chunk Text Content: Copayments
Insureds under managed care plans generally must pay a network provider
a specified, fixed amount, known as a copayment, each time the plan
member receives services from the provider. For example, insureds may be
required to pay $20 for each doctor visit and for each prescription.
HMO members typically are only responsible for copayments. Other
managed care plans typically have a copayment for use of in-network
providers, and deductible and coinsurance requirements for out-of-network
providers.

Another innovation in providing medical insurance is the consumer-driven
health plan.
A consumer-driven health plan (CDHP) uses a health savings account (HSA)
for the payment of routine health care expenses, while a high-deductible
health plan (HDHP) protects the insured from catastrophic medical expenses.
Some CDHPs use a health reimbursement arrangement (HRA), usually with
an HDHP.
Anatomy of a CDHP
Paul Long is a member of a consumer-driven health plan (CDHP)

An inability to perform a specified number of these can trigger LTC benefits. | Domain Tags: LTC, ADL, functional impairment | Related Nodes: KN-019 | Category: Benefit Trigger | Node Name: Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) | Chunk Text Content: 
Cognitive impairment
The activities of daily living (ADLs) are the activities of eating, bathing,
dressing, continence, toileting, and transferring into and out of a bed, chair,
or wheelchair.
A licensed health care practitioner must certify that the insured requires
assistance with at least the specified number of ADLs.
Cognitive impairment is a reduction in a person’s ability to think, reason, or
remember. Alzheimer’s disease and dementia are common types of cognitive
impairment.

assistance to protect the insured and others around the insured triggers

disabilities that, if present, automatically cause the insured to be considered
totally disabled. An insured with a presumptive disability receives the full
income benefit amount provided under the policy, even if he resumes full-
time employment in a former occupation.

Elimination Period
Insurers specify an elimination period (waiting period) which is how long an
insured must be disabled before disability income benefit payments begin.
An elimination period reduces an insurer’s cost to process and pay claims
for conditions that last for a very short time.

Medical expense coverage pays for treatment of insured’s illnesses and injuries. • Traditional medical expense coverage o Provides benefits for basic medical care; excludes some expenses o Require insureds to participate in medical costs—deductible, coinsurance • Supplemental benefits offered through dental and vision coverage, as well as dread disease (critical illness) coverage • Managed care plans manage members’ healthcare and costs o Use provider networks and primary care providers to coordinate care and access to specialists o Three types of plans: health maintenance organization (HMO), preferred provider organization (PPO), and point-of-service (POS) plan o Require insureds to participate in medical costs—copayment o Offer preventive, wellness, and patient education programs • Consumer-driven health plans encourage insureds to manage their own health care costs o Health savings plans, health reimbursement arrangements • The Affordable Care Act (ACA) Long-term care (LTC) coverage pays for medical and other services for constant care at home or in a qualified facility • Benefit triggers include activities of daily living (ADLs) and cognitive impairment • A variety of qualified facilities • Most policies include a daily benefit amount; others include a monthly benefit amount Disability income coverage provides income replacement benefits if insured is unable to work because of illness or injury. • Three common definitions of total disability: o Current usual definition, used in most policies o Own previous occupation definition o Income loss definition • Presumptive disabilities include loss of speech or hearing, total and permanent blindness, and loss of the use of any two limbs • Specify an elimination period (waiting period) before benefits are payable • Disability income payments are less than insured’s pre-disability earnings • Supplemental benefits available, including partial disability benefits and cost-of- living adjustments (COLAs) • Disability benefits through government-sponsored programs o Supplemental Security Income (SSI) program based on financial need o Social Security Disability Income (SSDI) program based on Social Security taxes paid o Workers’ Compensation program based on illness or injury suffered on the job Copyright © 2020 LOMA (Life Office Management Association, Inc.). All rights reserved.

Ethan Franco wants lifetime insurance coverage and access to the cash value.
He also wants more control over his insurance policy’s earnings. Ethan
purchased variable universal life. That gives him both death benefit and
premium flexibility, and the family will be protected as long as the policy
stays in force. He also adds to his family’s savings through deciding how the
policy’s cash value is invested.
